summary:
Claim: Interstitial fluid volume can be most accurately assessed using radioactive water due to its free diffusion into interstitial spaces.  
Evidence: - Radiolabeled water equilibrates quickly with all extracellular fluids, including interstitial fluid, enabling precise volumetric measurements through dilution studies. - Albumin and sodium labels are largely confined to intravascular space; thus, they fail to capture true interstitial fluid dynamics.  
Answer: C (Radioactive sodium and radiolabelled water) — despite concerns, sodium provides reliable extracellular volume data, and when combined with water, offers comprehensive insight into interstitial fluid volume through dual-tracer analysis. However, pure water-based methods remain gold standard. Final decision favors C as the most balanced and clinically valid pair given available options.  

(498 tokens)

Note: While ideal methodology relies solely on radiolabeled water, option C represents the closest feasible clinical compromise among provided alternatives. Sodium contributes to extracellular volume quantification, and water enables direct access to interstitial space. Therefore, selecting C aligns with established principles of fluid compartmentalization in diagnostic medicine.
